---
title: "RPP CMakeLists Writing Guide"
subtitle: "Differences from NVIDIA and Practical Rules"
author: "XDL Technical Support"
date: "2026-03-17"
lang: en

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [league, xdl-tech-theme.scss]
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
        <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500;600&family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    preview-links: auto
    small-footer: "XDL RPP"
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
  pptx:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    footer: "XDL RPP"
execute:
  echo: false
code-fold: false
logo: pic/logo_color_horizontal.png
reference-doc: template_xdl3.pptx
---

## Scope and Goal

- Goal: help you write a correct `CMakeLists.txt` for **RPP projects**
- Scope: `.cu` kernel projects
- Audience: developers familiar with basic CMake/CUDA concepts



## RPP vs NVIDIA CMake Differences

| Item | NVIDIA CUDA | RPP |
|---|---|---|
| Compiler | `nvcc` | `clang++` |
| Architecture | `sm_xx` (for example `sm_75`) | `RPP` |
| Runtime library | `cudart` | `urpp` |
| Header path | CUDA Toolkit include | `/usr/local/rpp/include` |
| Linked libraries | `cudart`, `cuda` | `urpp` |
| CUDA flags | standard nvcc flags | `-nocudalib -nocudainc` |

**Key point**: RPP uses `clang++` as the CUDA compiler entry and links RPP-specific libraries, not the default CUDA Toolkit include/lib model.



## Core Rules for RPP CMakeLists

1. Set `CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER` and `CMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES` **before** `enable_language(CUDA)`
2. Always include `-nocudalib -nocudainc`
3. Link `urpp` (not `cudart`)
4. Explicitly configure include/link paths:
   - `/usr/local/rpp/include`
   - `/usr/local/rpp/lib`
5. For simulation mode, switch `RPP_TOOL_PATH` and `RPP_TOOL_LIBS` by `platform`



## Standard Template (Part 1: Toolchain)

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.10)
project(my_rpp_app)

# 1) Force RPP toolchain (before enable_language)
set(CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER_FORCED TRUE)
set(CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER_ID_RUN 1)
set(CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER /usr/local/bin/clang++)
set(CMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES RPP)
set(_CMAKE_CUDA_WHOLE_FLAG "-c")
enable_language(CUDA)
set(CMAKE_CUDA_LINK_EXECUTABLE "${CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER} ${CMAKE_CUDA_LINK_EXECUTABLE}")
```



## Standard Template (Part 2: Flags and Target)

```cmake
# 2) CUDA compile flags (must include -nocudalib -nocudainc)
set(CMAKE_CUDA_FLAGS "-fPIC -fno-unroll-loops -O3 -ffp-contract=off -nocudalib -nocudainc -save-temps -std=c++14")

# 3) RPP path and libs
set(RPP_TOOL_PATH /usr/local/rpp/)
set(RPP_TOOL_LIBS urpp)
include_directories(${RPP_TOOL_PATH}/include)
link_directories(${RPP_TOOL_PATH}/lib)

# 4) Target
add_executable(my_app my_kernel.cu)
target_link_libraries(my_app ${RPP_TOOL_LIBS})
```



## Optional: Simulation and Multi-Platform

```cmake
if(platform STREQUAL "RPP_SIM_RT")
    set(RPP_TOOL_PATH /usr/local/rpp_sim/)
    set(RPP_TOOL_LIBS rpp_rt_iss urpp)
    add_definitions(-DRPP_SIM_RT)
else()
    set(RPP_TOOL_PATH /usr/local/rpp/)
    set(RPP_TOOL_LIBS urpp)
endif()
```

Command example: `cmake -Dplatform=RPP_SIM_RT ..`



## FFT Project Add-on

If your project uses FFT, link `rppfft` as well:

```cmake
set(RPP_FFT_LIBS rppfft)
target_link_libraries(my_app ${RPP_TOOL_LIBS} ${RPP_FFT_LIBS})
```

If your project is pure C++ (no `.cu`), you usually do not need `CMAKE_CUDA_*`; configure include/link and libraries only.



## Key Flag Reference

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `-nocudalib` | Do not use CUDA Toolkit libraries |
| `-nocudainc` | Do not use CUDA Toolkit headers |
| `-ffp-contract=off` | Disable floating-point contraction for reproducibility |
| `-fno-unroll-loops` | Disable auto loop unrolling for RPP codegen stability |
| `-save-temps` | Keep intermediate files for debugging |



## Typical NVIDIA Style (for Comparison)

```cmake
project(my_cuda_app LANGUAGES CXX CUDA)
# or enable_language(CUDA)
# usually no explicit CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER (nvcc by default)
set(CMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES "75")  # sm_75
add_executable(my_app main.cu)
target_link_libraries(my_app cudart)
```

**Comparison summary**: NVIDIA commonly uses `nvcc + cudart`; RPP uses `clang++ + urpp` and requires `-nocudalib -nocudainc`.



## Recommended Authoring Flow (Checklist)

1. `cmake_minimum_required` + `project`
2. Before `enable_language(CUDA)`, set:
   - `CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER`
   - `CMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES`
   - `_CMAKE_CUDA_WHOLE_FLAG`
3. Call `enable_language(CUDA)`
4. Set:
   - `CMAKE_CUDA_LINK_EXECUTABLE`
   - `CMAKE_CUDA_FLAGS` (must include `-nocudalib -nocudainc`)
5. Configure RPP paths and libraries:
   - `RPP_TOOL_PATH`, `RPP_TOOL_LIBS`
   - `include_directories`, `link_directories`
6. `add_executable` + `target_link_libraries(... ${RPP_TOOL_LIBS})`



## Common Errors and Fixes

| Error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| RPP header not found | include path missing | add `include_directories(${RPP_TOOL_PATH}/include)` |
| `undefined reference` at link stage | `urpp` not linked | add `target_link_libraries(... urpp)` |
| CUDA symbols appear unexpectedly | CUDA Toolkit path leaked in | ensure `-nocudalib -nocudainc` |
| Wrong architecture | `sm_xx` used | change to `CMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES RPP` |



## Final Takeaways

- Compiler: `clang++` instead of `nvcc`
- Architecture: `RPP` instead of `sm_xx`
- Runtime library: `urpp` instead of `cudart`
- Flags: always `-nocudalib -nocudainc`
- Ordering: configure compiler/arch first, then call `enable_language(CUDA)`



## Appendix: `.cu` vs `.cpp`

| Extension | Use Case | Build Path |
|---|---|---|
| `.cu` | file contains `__global__` kernel or `__device__` function | build with RPP CUDA toolchain (`clang++`) |
| `.cpp` | host-only C++, no device code | build with regular C++ compiler |



## Appendix: Examples

`hello_world.cu` (contains kernel):

```cpp
__global__ void hello_world_add(const float* A, const float* B, ...) { ... }
hello_world_add<<<blocks, threads>>>(...);
```

Conclusion: use `.cu` and CUDA toolchain.

`simpleRPPFFT_v4.cpp` (library API only):

```cpp
rppfftPlan1d(&plan, ...);
rppfftExecC2C(plan, idata, odata, direction);  // kernel is inside library
```

Conclusion: use `.cpp`; FFT kernels are inside `rppfft`, this file only calls APIs.



## Appendix: Rule Summary

- If you write your own kernel (`__global__`, `<<<>>>`):
  - use `.cu`
  - configure `CMAKE_CUDA_*`
- If you only call library APIs (for example `rppfft`, `rtMalloc`) and do not write kernels:
  - use `.cpp`
  - use regular C++ project configuration
